summary: This article reviews Ludwig Wittgenstein's 'Private Notebooks: 1914-1916', highlighting his dual life during World War I. It captures his early philosophical conclusions on the strict limits of language alongside his intense personal alienation and disgust toward his fellow soldiers.

principles.statement: There are absolute limits to what can be expressed; what falls outside these boundaries must be passed over in silence.
principles.rationale: Language has strict logical formulations, and attempting to articulate that which cannot be meaningfully said is impossible.
principles.source_quote: What cannot be said, cannot be said!

signature_quotes.text: Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent.
signature_quotes.context: The terse conclusion of his 'Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus'.

signature_quotes.text: What cannot be said, cannot be said!
signature_quotes.context: Written in his private notebook in July 1916.

signature_quotes.text: My shipmates are a bunch of swine! No enthusiasm for anything, unbelievable crudity, stupidity & malice
signature_quotes.context: Written a few days after enlisting in the Austrian army, expressing his disgust for his fellow soldiers.

anti_patterns.description: Exhibiting crudity, stupidity, malice, and a lack of enthusiasm.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Wittgenstein viewed the boorishness and insolence of ordinary people (such as his fellow soldiers) with intense disgust and revulsion, seeing it as a form of viciousness.
